question_text: The sacrificing ratio is calculated as:
explanation: Sacrificing Ratio = Old Ratio − New Ratio. A partner 'sacrifices' when their new share is less than their old share. The sacrificing ratio determines how much each partner has given up in favour of another (usually the incoming partner during admission, or a partner whose share increases during a change in PSR).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: New Ratio − Old Ratio gives the GAINING ratio, not the sacrificing ratio. If the result is positive, the partner gained; the partner who sacrificed has Old > New.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the gaining ratio formula with the sacrificing ratio formula.

elimination_hints.hint: Multiplication of ratios has no meaning in this context. Sacrificing is about the difference between old and new shares.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying an incorrect mathematical operation to the ratios.

elimination_hints.hint: Adding old and new ratios does not give any meaningful result. Sacrifice = Old − New (how much a partner gave up).
elimination_hints.misconception: Adding ratios instead of finding the difference.

question_text: A, B and C share profits in 3:2:1. They decide to share equally. The gaining ratio is:
explanation:
Old ratio: A = 3/6, B = 2/6, C = 1/6. New ratio: A = 1/3 = 2/6, B = 1/3 = 2/6, C = 1/3 = 2/6.
Gaining Ratio = New − Old: A = 2/6 − 3/6 = −1/6 (sacrificed). B = 2/6 − 2/6 = 0 (neither). C = 2/6 − 1/6 = 1/6 (gained).
Only C gained, in the ratio 1/6. So the gaining ratio is C alone gaining 1/6, which A sacrificed.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: A's old share = 3/6, new = 2/6. A has LOST 1/6 (sacrificed), not gained. Check: gaining = new − old.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing sacrificing with gaining — A went from 3/6 to 2/6 which is a decrease.

elimination_hints.hint: B's old share = 2/6, new = 2/6. B neither gained nor sacrificed (change = 0). Only C gained.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming B gained when B's share remained unchanged.

elimination_hints.hint: A sacrificed 1/6 and B's share is unchanged. Neither A nor B gained. Only C moved from 1/6 to 2/6.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not computing the individual differences between old and new shares.

question_text: When the profit-sharing ratio changes among existing partners, which account is prepared to revalue assets and liabilities?
explanation: A Revaluation Account is prepared when the profit-sharing ratio changes. It records the increase or decrease in the values of assets and liabilities. Increases in assets and decreases in liabilities are credited (gains), while decreases in assets and increases in liabilities are debited (losses). The net profit or loss on revaluation is transferred to all partners' capital accounts in the OLD profit-sharing ratio.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: P&L Account records regular business income and expenses. Revaluation of assets/liabilities during reconstitution is recorded in a separate Revaluation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the regular P&L Account for non-recurring revaluation adjustments.

elimination_hints.hint: Realisation Account is prepared during DISSOLUTION of a firm, not during a change in PSR. It records the disposal of assets and settlement of liabilities.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing reconstitution (ongoing firm) with dissolution (closing firm).

elimination_hints.hint: Memorandum Revaluation Account is used when partners do NOT want to change the book values of assets/liabilities. The regular Revaluation Account actually changes book values.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the standard Revaluation Account with the Memorandum Revaluation Account.

question_text: X, Y and Z share profits in 5:3:2. They decide to share in 2:3:5. The sacrificing and gaining ratios are:
explanation:
Old: X = 5/10, Y = 3/10, Z = 2/10. New: X = 2/10, Y = 3/10, Z = 5/10.
Difference: X = 2/10 − 5/10 = −3/10 (sacrificed 3/10). Y = 3/10 − 3/10 = 0. Z = 5/10 − 2/10 = +3/10 (gained 3/10).
Sacrificing: X sacrificed 3/10. Gaining: Z gained 3/10. Y is unaffected.
subject: accountancy

options.text: X and Y sacrifice equally; Z gains
X sacrifices 3/10; Z gains 3/10; Y unchanged

elimination_hints.hint: Y's share didn't change (3/10 → 3/10). Y neither sacrificed nor gained. Only X sacrificed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming multiple partners sacrificed when only one partner's share decreased.

elimination_hints.hint: X went from 5/10 to 2/10 — a decrease of 3/10, not 1/10. Similarly Z gained 3/10.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in computing the difference between old and new ratios.

elimination_hints.hint: Y didn't sacrifice (Y stayed at 3/10). X sacrificed (5/10 → 2/10) and Z gained (2/10 → 5/10).
elimination_hints.misconception: Picking the wrong partners for sacrifice and gain.

question_text: The profit or loss on revaluation of assets and liabilities at the time of change in PSR is transferred to partners' capital accounts in:
explanation: When the profit-sharing ratio changes, the revaluation profit or loss is transferred to ALL partners' capital accounts in their OLD profit-sharing ratio. This is because the change in asset/liability values occurred during the period when partners were sharing profits in the old ratio. The old ratio reflects their entitlement to any gains or losses that arose before the change.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio applies AFTER the change. The revaluation relates to the period BEFORE the change, so old ratio is used.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the new ratio to events that occurred under the old arrangement.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrificing ratio is used for goodwill adjustments (to determine who compensates whom), not for distributing revaluation profit/loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the purpose of sacrificing ratio with the distribution of revaluation gains/losses.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital ratio is not used unless specifically stated. The standard rule is to use the old PSR for revaluation entries.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming capital ratio governs revaluation distribution.

question_text: P, Q and R share profits in 4:3:3. They decide to share in 3:3:4. The firm has a General Reserve of ₹90,000 shown in the Balance Sheet. The General Reserve will be:
explanation: When the PSR changes, accumulated reserves (like General Reserve, Profit & Loss balance) must be distributed among partners in the OLD ratio before the new ratio takes effect. This is because these reserves were built up during the period of the old ratio. General Reserve ₹90,000 distributed in 4:3:3: P = ₹36,000, Q = ₹27,000, R = ₹27,000. Journal entry: Dr. General Reserve ₹90,000, Cr. P's Capital ₹36,000, Cr. Q's Capital ₹27,000, Cr. R's Capital ₹27,000.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subtopic: Distribution of reserves on change in PSR
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio applies going forward. The reserve was accumulated under the old ratio, so it belongs to partners in that ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the new ratio for reserves earned under the old arrangement.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrificing ratio determines goodwill compensation, not reserve distribution. Reserves are distributed in old PSR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying sacrificing ratio to all adjustments during reconstitution.

elimination_hints.hint: Carrying forward without distribution would be unfair — the partner who sacrificed share would lose their proportionate claim on reserves earned under the old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the need to settle accumulated reserves before changing the ratio.

question_text: On a change in profit-sharing ratio, if an asset's book value is ₹80,000 and its revalued amount is ₹1,00,000, the Revaluation Account will be:
explanation: The asset has increased in value from ₹80,000 to ₹1,00,000 — an increase of ₹20,000. An increase in asset value is a gain for the firm. In the Revaluation Account: the asset account is debited (to increase its value) and the Revaluation Account is credited ₹20,000. Credit to Revaluation Account = gain on revaluation.
subtopic: Treatment of increase in asset value
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,00,000 is the new value, not the change. Only the increase of ₹20,000 is recorded in the Revaluation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Recording the entire new value instead of the change in value.

elimination_hints.hint: The Revaluation Account is CREDITED (not debited) for an increase in asset value, as it represents a gain.
elimination_hints.misconception: Debiting instead of crediting for an increase in asset — confusing debit/credit sides.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹80,000 is the old book value. The Revaluation Account records only the difference (₹20,000), not the old or new absolute value.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the old book value instead of the change amount.

question_text: A, B and C share profits in 3:2:1. They decide to share in 2:2:2. Goodwill of the firm is valued at ₹1,20,000. The journal entry for goodwill adjustment (without raising goodwill in books) will be:
explanation:
Old: A=3/6, B=2/6, C=1/6. New: 2:2:2 = 1/3 each = 2/6 each.
Sacrifice/Gain: A = 2/6−3/6 = −1/6 (sacrificed). B = 2/6−2/6 = 0. C = 2/6−1/6 = +1/6 (gained).
Goodwill = ₹1,20,000. C (gainer) compensates A (sacrificer).
C pays A: 1,20,000 × 1/6 = ₹20,000.
Entry: Debit C's Capital ₹20,000, Credit A's Capital ₹20,000.
subtopic: Goodwill adjustment without raising in books
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.misconception: Multiplying goodwill by an incorrect fraction instead of the gain/sacrifice fraction.

elimination_hints.hint: B's share didn't change (2/6 → 2/6). B neither gained nor sacrificed, so B should not be debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Debiting B even though B's share is unchanged.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹40,000 = 1,20,000 × 2/6. This uses C's new share instead of C's gain (1/6). Compensation = Goodwill × Gain fraction = 1,20,000 × 1/6.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the new share fraction instead of the gain fraction for goodwill compensation.

question_text: When goodwill already appears in the books at the time of change in PSR, it should be:
explanation: When goodwill already appears in the old Balance Sheet, it must be written off by debiting all partners' capital accounts in the OLD ratio before any new goodwill adjustment is made. This ensures the old goodwill (recorded under previous conditions) is removed. Entry: Dr. All Partners' Capital A/cs (old ratio), Cr. Goodwill A/c. After writing off, the new goodwill adjustment (if any) is made separately.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Written off in the new ratio
Written off in the sacrificing ratio
Written off in the old ratio

elimination_hints.hint: New ratio applies after the change. The old goodwill was built under the old arrangement and must be written off in the old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the new ratio for adjustments related to the pre-change period.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrificing ratio is used for new goodwill compensation between gainers and sacrificers. Existing goodwill write-off uses the old PSR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the ratio used for existing goodwill removal with the ratio for new goodwill adjustment.

elimination_hints.hint: Carrying forward would be incorrect because the goodwill was established under different conditions. It must be written off before new adjustments are made.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the need to remove old goodwill before reconstitution adjustments.

question_text: D, E and F share profits in 5:3:2. They agree to share in 3:4:3. On revaluation: Land increased by ₹50,000, Machinery decreased by ₹20,000, Creditors were overstated by ₹10,000, and a provision for doubtful debts of ₹8,000 was to be created. The net revaluation profit/loss is:
explanation:
Revaluation Account:
Credit (gains): Land increase ₹50,000 + Creditors overstatement written back ₹10,000 = ₹60,000.
Debit (losses): Machinery decrease ₹20,000 + Provision for doubtful debts ₹8,000 = ₹28,000.
Net Revaluation Profit = ₹60,000 − ₹28,000 = ₹32,000 (Profit).
Note: Creditors overstated means the liability was shown higher than actual — reducing it is a gain. Creating provision for doubtful debts is a new liability/expense.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: The gains (₹60,000) exceed the losses (₹28,000), resulting in a net PROFIT, not a loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing gains and losses — putting increases on the debit side and decreases on credit.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹22,000 might result from not including the creditors adjustment (₹10,000). Overstated creditors means liability was too high — reducing it is a gain (credit to Revaluation Account).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing that overstated creditors correction is a gain.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹42,000 might result from not deducting the provision for doubtful debts (₹8,000). Creating a new provision is a loss (debit to Revaluation Account).
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to debit the provision for doubtful debts as a loss.

question_text: A Memorandum Revaluation Account is prepared when partners want to:
explanation: A Memorandum Revaluation Account is prepared when the partners want to record the effect of revaluation on their capital accounts but do NOT want to change the actual book values of assets and liabilities. It has two parts: Part I records the revaluation adjustments (same as a regular Revaluation Account) and distributes net effect in OLD ratio. Part II reverses all the entries and distributes in NEW ratio. The net effect adjusts capital accounts without altering the Balance Sheet values.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Permanently change the book values of assets
Record revaluation effect without changing actual book values

elimination_hints.hint: Permanently changing book values is done by a regular Revaluation Account. Memorandum Revaluation Account specifically avoids changing book values.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing regular Revaluation Account with Memorandum Revaluation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: Dissolution uses a Realisation Account. Memorandum Revaluation is used during reconstitution (change in PSR, admission, retirement) when the firm continues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing reconstitution with dissolution.

elimination_hints.hint: Memorandum Revaluation Account deals with assets and liabilities, not goodwill. Goodwill has its own separate treatment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up revaluation of tangible assets with goodwill treatment.

question_text: L, M and N share profits in 1:2:3. They decide to share equally. The Balance Sheet shows: P&L Account (Cr.) ₹60,000, General Reserve ₹30,000, and Goodwill ₹24,000. The net effect on L's Capital Account (total of all adjustments) is:
explanation:
Old: L=1/6, M=2/6, N=3/6. New: 1/3 each = 2/6 each.
1. P&L (Cr.) ₹60,000 distributed in old ratio: L = 60,000×1/6 = ₹10,000 (Cr).
2. General Reserve ₹30,000 distributed in old ratio: L = 30,000×1/6 = ₹5,000 (Cr).
3. Existing Goodwill ₹24,000 written off in old ratio: L = 24,000×1/6 = ₹4,000 (Dr).
4. Goodwill adjustment (new goodwill not raised): L's sacrifice/gain = 2/6−1/6 = +1/6 (gained). But we need to consider if new goodwill is raised. Question doesn't mention new goodwill valuation, so only existing adjustments.
Net effect on L = +10,000 + 5,000 − 4,000 = ₹11,000 Credit.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹15,000 = P&L share (10,000) + Reserve share (5,000). This ignores the goodwill write-off of ₹4,000 which is debited to L's account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to deduct goodwill write-off from the total credits.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,000 is only the goodwill write-off debit. L also receives ₹10,000 + ₹5,000 as credits from P&L and reserves. Net = 11,000 credit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only the goodwill write-off and ignoring reserve/P&L distributions.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹19,000 might include goodwill write-off as a credit instead of debit. Writing off goodwill reduces capital (debit), not increases it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating goodwill write-off as a credit instead of a debit to partner's capital.

question_text:
Assertion (A): On a change in profit-sharing ratio, the gaining partner compensates the sacrificing partner for goodwill.
Reason (R): The sacrificing partner is giving up a portion of their future profit share, and the goodwill payment compensates for this loss.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — the gaining partner compensates the sacrificing partner by paying for goodwill in the gaining/sacrificing ratio. The Reason is also TRUE — the sacrificing partner gives up future profit share, and goodwill represents the value of future super profits that the partner would have earned. The Reason correctly explains the Assertion — because the sacrificing partner loses future profits, they receive goodwill compensation from the gainer.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY the gainer compensates — because the sacrificer loses future profits. This is the exact rationale behind goodwill adjustments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the direct causal link between loss of future profits and goodwill compensation.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — goodwill does represent future earning capacity, and the sacrificing partner is indeed losing a share of future profits.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the economic rationale behind goodwill adjustments.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — gaining partners do compensate sacrificing partners for goodwill. This is a fundamental principle of partnership reconstitution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the basic rule of goodwill compensation during PSR changes.

question_text:
Assertion (A): In a Memorandum Revaluation Account, Part I is distributed in the old ratio and Part II is distributed in the new ratio.
Reason (R): Part I records the revaluation adjustments and Part II reverses them, so that the book values remain unchanged but capital accounts are correctly adjusted.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — Part I of the Memorandum Revaluation Account distributes the revaluation profit/loss in the OLD ratio, and Part II reverses these entries and distributes in the NEW ratio. The Reason is also TRUE — Part I records the same entries as a regular Revaluation Account, and Part II reverses them so that actual book values of assets/liabilities remain unchanged. The net effect correctly adjusts partners' capitals. R correctly explains A.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains the mechanism of the two parts and WHY they use different ratios — to adjust capitals without changing book values. This is the core explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the reversal mechanism with the dual-ratio distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: R IS true — the two-part mechanism (record then reverse) is exactly how Memorandum Revaluation Account maintains unchanged book values.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding the reversal concept of Memorandum Revaluation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: A IS true — this is the standard structure: Part I in old ratio, Part II in new ratio. This is the defining characteristic of the Memorandum Revaluation Account.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the basic structure of the Memorandum Revaluation Account.

question_text: When the profit-sharing ratio changes, accumulated reserves and undistributed profits must be distributed among partners in the old ratio before applying the new ratio.
explanation: TRUE. Accumulated reserves (General Reserve, Reserve Fund) and undistributed profits (P&L Account credit balance) were built up during the period when partners shared profits in the old ratio. Therefore, these must be distributed in the old ratio to ensure fairness. If not distributed, the partner who gained a larger share would benefit from reserves they didn't fully help accumulate.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: If reserves are not distributed in the old ratio, partners who sacrificed their share would lose their rightful claim on accumulated profits. The old ratio must be used.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking reserves can be carried forward without distribution during a change in PSR.

question_text: The gaining ratio and sacrificing ratio always add up to zero across all partners.
explanation: TRUE. The total of all gains must equal the total of all sacrifices. If one partner's share increases by 1/6, another partner's share must decrease by 1/6. The total profit share is always 1 (or 100%), so any increase for one partner must be exactly offset by decreases for other partners. Mathematically: Sum of (New Ratio − Old Ratio) for all partners = (sum of new ratios) − (sum of old ratios) = 1 − 1 = 0.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Total shares always sum to 1. If someone gains, someone else must sacrifice by the exact same amount. It's a zero-sum change.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that profit shares are a fixed total (1 or 100%) that gets redistributed.

question_text:
Match the following items with the ratio in which they are distributed/adjusted during a change in PSR:

Column I:
(i) General Reserve
(ii) Revaluation Profit/Loss
(iii) Goodwill compensation between partners
(iv) Part II of Memorandum Revaluation Account

Column II:
(P) Old Profit-Sharing Ratio
(Q) Sacrificing/Gaining Ratio
(R) New Profit-Sharing Ratio
(S) Old Profit-Sharing Ratio
explanation: (i) General Reserve → (P) Old PSR: Reserves accumulated under old arrangement. (ii) Revaluation Profit/Loss → (S) Old PSR: Changes in asset values relate to pre-change period. (iii) Goodwill compensation → (Q) Sacrificing/Gaining Ratio: Gainers compensate sacrificers. (iv) Part II of Memorandum Revaluation Account → (R) New PSR: The reversal entries are distributed in new ratio to leave net effect on capitals.
topic: Treatment of Reserves and Accumulated Profits
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns Revaluation Profit (ii) to new ratio (R). But revaluation relates to the pre-change period, so old ratio (S) is correct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using new ratio for revaluation that occurred under the old arrangement.

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns General Reserve (i) to sacrificing/gaining ratio (Q). Reserves are distributed in old PSR (P), not in the sacrifice/gain ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing reserve distribution with goodwill compensation — different ratios for different purposes.

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns General Reserve (i) to new ratio (R) and goodwill (iii) to old ratio (P). Both are wrong — reserves use old ratio, goodwill uses sacrifice/gain ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping multiple ratios — not understanding which adjustment uses which ratio.

question_text: Gaining Ratio = _______ − Old Ratio. If the result is negative, the partner has sacrificed.
explanation: Gaining Ratio = New Ratio − Old Ratio. If a partner's new share exceeds their old share, they have gained. If the new share is less than the old share, the result is negative, meaning the partner has sacrificed. The gaining partners compensate the sacrificing partners for goodwill.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Old Ratio − Old Ratio = 0 always. The formula compares NEW share with old share to find the gain.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using old ratio on both sides of the formula.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital Ratio is about capital contributions, not about the change in profit-sharing. Gaining ratio compares new PSR with old PSR.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing capital ratio with the profit-sharing ratio changes.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrifice Ratio = Old − New. Gaining Ratio = New − Old. They are opposites of each other.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the gaining ratio with the sacrificing ratio.

question_text: G, H and I are partners sharing in 5:4:1. They decide to share equally. Balance Sheet shows: General Reserve ₹60,000, P&L A/c (Dr.) ₹15,000, Goodwill ₹30,000. On revaluation: Building increased by ₹40,000, Stock decreased by ₹10,000, Unrecorded liability of ₹6,000 discovered. The net effect on G's Capital Account is:
explanation:
Old: G=5/10, H=4/10, I=1/10. New: equal = 1/3 each.
1. General Reserve ₹60,000 in old ratio: G = 60,000 × 5/10 = ₹30,000 (Cr).
2. P&L (Dr.) ₹15,000 (loss) in old ratio: G = 15,000 × 5/10 = ₹7,500 (Dr).
3. Existing Goodwill ₹30,000 written off in old ratio: G = 30,000 × 5/10 = ₹15,000 (Dr).
4. Revaluation: Credits: Building +40,000 = ₹40,000. Debits: Stock −10,000 + Liability 6,000 = ₹16,000. Net profit = ₹24,000. G's share (old ratio) = 24,000 × 5/10 = ₹12,000 (Cr).
Net on G = +30,000 − 7,500 − 15,000 + 12,000 = ₹19,500 Credit.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹30,000 is only G's share of General Reserve. It ignores the P&L debit (₹7,500), goodwill write-off (₹15,000), and revaluation profit (₹12,000).
elimination_hints.misconception: Only accounting for one adjustment and ignoring the rest.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹7,500 is only G's share of the P&L debit balance. The total net effect includes multiple credits and debits.
elimination_hints.misconception: Focusing on only one adjustment item.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹42,000 = 30,000 + 12,000. This includes reserve and revaluation credits but ignores the P&L debit (₹7,500) and goodwill write-off (₹15,000).
elimination_hints.misconception: Including only the credit items and ignoring the debit items.

question_text:
Arrange the steps for accounting treatment when there is a change in profit-sharing ratio:
(i) Adjust goodwill (compensate sacrificers by gainers)
(ii) Distribute accumulated reserves and undistributed profits in old ratio
(iii) Prepare Revaluation Account and transfer profit/loss in old ratio
(iv) Write off existing goodwill in old ratio (if any)
(v) Prepare new Balance Sheet with adjusted figures
explanation: Correct order: (1) Write off existing goodwill in old ratio (iv) — remove old goodwill first. (2) Distribute accumulated reserves in old ratio (ii) — settle old claims. (3) Prepare Revaluation Account in old ratio (iii) — adjust asset/liability values. (4) Adjust new goodwill between partners (i) — compensate sacrifice/gain. (5) Prepare new Balance Sheet (v) — final step. Order: (iv) → (ii) → (iii) → (i) → (v).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This adjusts new goodwill (i) before writing off existing goodwill (iv). Existing goodwill must be removed first before any new goodwill adjustment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Adjusting new goodwill before clearing old goodwill from the books.

elimination_hints.hint: Starting with goodwill adjustment (i) before writing off existing goodwill (iv) and distributing reserves (ii) is incorrect. Old matters must be settled first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not clearing prior-period items before making new adjustments.

elimination_hints.hint: This places the new Balance Sheet (v) before goodwill adjustment (i). All adjustments must be completed before preparing the final Balance Sheet.
elimination_hints.misconception: Preparing the Balance Sheet before completing all reconstitution adjustments.
